question: A patient from a rural endemic area presents with new-onset focal seizures and headaches years after immigration. Neuroimaging shows multiple calcified intracranial cystic lesions. Which of the following is the most likely etiologic agent?
cited_learning_objective: Explain the transmission, pathogenesis, clinical manifestation, diagnosis, & treatment of infections caused by Taenia solium.

options.text: Larval stage of the pork tapeworm Taenia solium
options.rationale: Neurocysticercosis arises when humans harbor larval cysticerci of Taenia solium in brain tissue, causing seizures when cysts calcify and provoke inflammation.

options.text: Adult beef tapeworm Taenia saginata in intestine
options.rationale: T. saginata causes intestinal taeniasis but does not invade tissues; neurocysticercosis requires larval invasion by T. solium eggs.

options.text: Encysted larvae of Toxoplasma gondii in brain
options.rationale: Toxoplasma causes ring-enhancing lesions in immunocompromised hosts; calcified cysts with new focal seizures in immigrants more specifically indicate Taenia solium.

options.text: Encapsulated yeast Cryptococcus neoformans infection
options.rationale: Cryptococcus causes meningitis with encapsulated yeast on India ink and different clinical picture; it does not produce calcified parenchymal cysts causing focal seizures.

options.text: Prion disease causing spongiform cortical change
options.rationale: Prion diseases produce rapidly progressive dementia and spongiform change, not focal calcified cysts or delayed-onset focal seizures.

question: Which diagnostic test most directly visualizes the causative intracranial lesions in the condition caused by ingestion of the organism discussed in the previous question?
cited_learning_objective: Compare & contrast the transmission, diagnosis, & treatment of taeniasis vs. neurocysticercosis.

options.text: Computed tomography or magnetic resonance imaging
options.rationale: Imaging (CT/MRI) detects cysts and calcified lesions of neurocysticercosis, the primary diagnostic modality for intracranial cysticerci.

options.text: Stool ova and parasite examination for eggs
options.rationale: Stool exam diagnoses intestinal taeniasis by detecting eggs or proglottids but may be negative in isolated neurocysticercosis without intestinal infection.

options.text: India ink staining of cerebrospinal fluid
options.rationale: India ink identifies encapsulated yeast like Cryptococcus in CSF, not cysticerci; it would not visualize parenchymal cysts.

options.text: Serologic antibody testing for parasite antigens
options.rationale: Serology can support neurocysticercosis diagnosis but may be negative in light infections; imaging is more direct for intracranial lesions.

options.text: Brain biopsy with histologic identification of larvae
options.rationale: Biopsy is definitive but invasive and reserved for atypical cases; CT/MRI is the primary, less invasive diagnostic test.

question: A household member of a patient with intestinal tapeworm infection asks how to prevent others from developing tissue-invasive disease. Which single measure best reduces the risk of household transmission leading to tissue invasion?
cited_learning_objective: Compare & contrast the transmission, diagnosis, & treatment of taeniasis vs. neurocysticercosis.

options.text: Improve sanitation and hand hygiene to prevent fecal contamination
options.rationale: Neurocysticercosis results from ingestion of eggs via fecal-oral contamination; sanitation and hand hygiene interrupt egg transmission.

options.text: Thoroughly cook pork to internal safe temperature
options.rationale: Cooking pork prevents taeniasis from ingesting cysticerci but does not prevent egg-mediated cysticercosis transmitted via fecal contamination.

options.text: Freeze pork at −20°C for at least twelve hours
options.rationale: Freezing pork prevents cysticerci survival and taeniasis but does not address fecal-oral egg transmission that causes neurocysticercosis.

options.text: Treat all household members with praziquantel empirically
options.rationale: Treating carriers can reduce egg shedding but sanitation and hygiene are primary preventive measures; empirical treatment is not the single best immediate measure.

options.text: Avoid consumption of undercooked beef in the household
options.rationale: Undercooked beef risks T. saginata taeniasis but T. saginata does not cause tissue-invasive disease; sanitation is more relevant to preventing cysticercosis.

question: Which antiparasitic drug acts by binding parasite β-tubulin and inhibiting microtubule polymerization, thereby disrupting glucose uptake in helminths?
cited_learning_objective: Explain the transmission, pathogenesis, clinical manifestation, diagnosis, & treatment of infections caused by Taenia solium.

options.text: Albendazole, a broad-spectrum anti-helminthic agent
options.rationale: Albendazole binds β-tubulin and inhibits tubulin polymerization, disrupting glucose uptake and cellular structure in helminths as described in the lecture.

options.text: Praziquantel, which increases membrane calcium permeability
options.rationale: Praziquantel causes worm paralysis via increased calcium permeability; it does not act on β-tubulin or microtubules.

options.text: Pyrimethamine, an inhibitor of dihydrofolate reductase
options.rationale: Pyrimethamine inhibits folate synthesis used for Toxoplasma treatment; it does not target helminth β-tubulin microtubules.

options.text: Amphotericin B, which binds ergosterol in fungal membranes
options.rationale: Amphotericin B targets fungal ergosterol and is not an anti-helminthic acting on β-tubulin.

options.text: Flucytosine, which inhibits fungal DNA and protein synthesis
options.rationale: Flucytosine is an antifungal that inhibits nucleic acid synthesis and does not disrupt helminth microtubules.

question: A previously healthy swimmer develops abrupt severe frontal headache, high fever, and rapidly progressive meningoencephalitis after diving in warm freshwater. Which route of entry best explains how the causative organism reached the central nervous system?
cited_learning_objective: Explain the transmission, pathogenesis, clinical manifestation, & diagnosis of infections caused by Naegleria fowleri.

options.text: Direct nasal mucosa invasion with migration along olfactory nerves
options.rationale: Naegleria fowleri enters via the nasal mucosa and migrates along olfactory nerves to the brain, causing primary amoebic meningoencephalitis.

options.text: Hematogenous spread after pulmonary inhalation of cysts
options.rationale: Naegleria fowleri does not typically disseminate hematogenously from lungs; it gains CNS access via nasal-olfactory route.

options.text: Transplacental transmission during maternal infection
options.rationale: Transplacental spread is not a recognized route for Naegleria fowleri; the organism infects via nasal exposure in recreational water.

options.text: Peripheral nerve retrograde transport from skin wounds
options.rationale: Peripheral nerve spread from skin wounds is not the mechanism; olfactory nerve migration from nasal mucosa is the key route.

options.text: Ingestion of contaminated water with intestinal invasion
options.rationale: Ingestion does not lead to CNS infection by Naegleria fowleri; nasal exposure is required for CNS invasion.

question: Which CSF diagnostic method is most likely to demonstrate the motile trophozoite form of the organism causing the rapidly fatal freshwater-associated meningoencephalitis?
cited_learning_objective: Explain the transmission, pathogenesis, clinical manifestation, & diagnosis of infections caused by Naegleria fowleri.

options.text: Wet mount microscopy of cerebrospinal fluid for motile trophozoites
options.rationale: Wet mount of CSF can reveal motile Naegleria trophozoites, the diagnostic form in CSF as noted in the lecture.

options.text: India ink stain to visualize encapsulated organisms
options.rationale: India ink identifies encapsulated yeast like Cryptococcus, not Naegleria trophozoites.

options.text: Acid-fast staining for mycobacterial organisms
options.rationale: Acid-fast stains detect mycobacteria and are not useful for free-living amoebae like Naegleria.

options.text: Silver stain to detect fungal hyphae in CSF
options.rationale: Silver stains detect fungal elements such as Pneumocystis, not Naegleria trophozoites.

options.text: Serologic antibody testing for amoebic antigens
options.rationale: Serology is not the primary rapid diagnostic for Naegleria; direct visualization or nucleic acid detection from CSF is preferred.

question: A clinician asks which immediate therapeutic approach is recommended for suspected primary amoebic meningoencephalitis. Which of the following best reflects the lecture's recommended initial management?
cited_learning_objective: Explain the transmission, pathogenesis, clinical manifestation, & diagnosis of infections caused by Naegleria fowleri.

options.text: Immediate combination antimicrobial therapy and contact CDC
options.rationale: Lecture emphasizes immediate combination therapy (amphotericin B, fluconazole, rifampin, azithromycin, miltefosine, dexamethasone) and contacting CDC for Naegleria cases.

options.text: Delay therapy until culture confirmation of organism
options.rationale: Delaying therapy is inappropriate because Naegleria causes rapid fatal disease; immediate empiric combination therapy is recommended.

options.text: High-dose fluconazole monotherapy alone
options.rationale: Monotherapy with fluconazole is insufficient; lecture recommends combination regimens including amphotericin B and miltefosine.

options.text: Supportive care only without antimicrobials
options.rationale: Supportive care alone is inadequate for Naegleria infection; aggressive antimicrobial therapy is indicated despite poor prognosis.

options.text: Oral albendazole and praziquantel for helminthic coverage
options.rationale: Albendazole and praziquantel target helminths and are not appropriate for free-living amoebae like Naegleria fowleri.

question: Which of the following best summarizes the structural difference between the normal cellular prion protein and the disease-associated isoform?
cited_learning_objective: Define the basic biochemical and biological properties of prions and compare the structural and functional significances of PrPc and PrPSc.

options.text: PrPSc has increased beta-pleated sheets versus alpha-helices
options.rationale: Lecture states PrPSc is enriched in β-pleated sheets and multimeric, whereas PrPc has more α-helices and is monomeric.

options.text: PrPc is protease resistant and forms amyloid fibrils
options.rationale: PrPc is protease sensitive and does not form scrapie fibrils; protease resistance and fibril formation characterize PrPSc.

options.text: PrPSc is monomeric with rapid cellular turnover
options.rationale: PrPSc is multimeric with slower turnover (days); PrPc is monomeric with faster turnover (hours).

options.text: PrPc is secreted extracellularly and accumulates in plaques
options.rationale: PrPc is located on the plasma membrane and is not present in scrapie fibrils; extracellular plaque accumulation is a feature of PrPSc.

options.text: PrPSc contains unique amino acid sequence absent in PrPc
options.rationale: PrPSc and PrPc share identical primary sequence; they differ in tertiary structure, not amino acid sequence.

question: A patient develops rapidly progressive dementia with myoclonus over months. CSF shows elevated 14-3-3 protein. Which diagnostic step provides definitive confirmation according to the lecture?
cited_learning_objective: Describe and contrast, when appropriate, the principle pathological findings, clinical manifestation, diagnosis, and epidemiology of the three major types of human prion diseases: sporadic, genetic, & acquired.

options.text: Brain biopsy or autopsy demonstrating spongiform change
options.rationale: Definitive diagnosis requires neuropathologic examination showing amyloid plaques, spongiform vacuoles, and PrP staining on brain tissue.

options.text: Positive serum treponemal antibody confirming infection
options.rationale: Treponemal serology diagnoses syphilis, not prion disease; prion diagnosis relies on neuropathology or specialized assays like RT-QuIC.

options.text: India ink positive cerebrospinal fluid demonstrating yeast
options.rationale: India ink detects encapsulated yeast (Cryptococcus), not prion proteins; it is irrelevant to prion confirmation.

options.text: PCR detection of prion DNA in blood sample
options.rationale: Prions are proteinaceous agents without nucleic acid; PCR for DNA is not applicable for prion detection.

options.text: Elevated CSF protein alone without tissue examination
options.rationale: CSF markers like 14-3-3 are supportive but nonspecific; definitive diagnosis requires brain tissue examination.

question: Which clinical feature is most characteristic of sporadic prion disease as emphasized in the lecture?
cited_learning_objective: Describe and contrast, when appropriate, the principle pathological findings, clinical manifestation, diagnosis, and epidemiology of the three major types of human prion diseases: sporadic, genetic, & acquired.

options.text: Rapidly progressive dementia with stimulus-induced myoclonus
options.rationale: Sporadic CJD commonly presents with rapidly progressive dementia and myoclonus often triggered by noise or movement, as noted in the lecture.

options.text: Insomnia and autonomic failure as earliest signs
options.rationale: Fatal familial insomnia presents with severe sleep disturbance and autonomic dysfunction; this is a genetic prion syndrome, not the typical sporadic form.

options.text: Long incubation with cerebellar ataxia from cannibalism exposure
options.rationale: Kuru from ritualistic cannibalism has long incubation and cerebellar signs; this describes an acquired prion disease, not sporadic CJD.

options.text: Young adult psychiatric prodrome with prolonged course
options.rationale: Variant CJD often affects younger patients with psychiatric symptoms; sporadic CJD typically affects older adults with rapid decline.

options.text: Slowly progressive peripheral neuropathy over decades
options.rationale: Peripheral neuropathy over decades is not characteristic of sporadic prion disease, which progresses rapidly with central neurodegeneration.

question: A patient with advanced HIV (CD4 50 cells/uL) presents with headache and meningismus. India ink stain of CSF reveals encapsulated yeast. Which organism is most consistent with these findings and the lecture content?
cited_learning_objective: Describe the physiological properties, virulence factors, and transmission of Cryptococcus spp.

options.text: Cryptococcus neoformans acquired from bird-contaminated soil
options.rationale: C. neoformans, associated with bird droppings, causes meningitis in immunocompromised patients and is identified by India ink showing a thick capsule.

options.text: Cryptococcus gattii acquired from eucalyptus tree environments
options.rationale: C. gattii can infect immunocompetent hosts and is associated with trees; India ink findings are similar but C. neoformans is classically linked to AIDS patients.

options.text: Candida albicans causing mucosal thrush and systemic disease
options.rationale: Candida can cause systemic disease but India ink capsule visualization is specific for encapsulated Cryptococcus species, not Candida.

options.text: Pneumocystis jirovecii causing interstitial pneumonia
options.rationale: Pneumocystis causes pneumonia in AIDS and is diagnosed by silver stain of lung specimens, not India ink of CSF.

options.text: Histoplasma capsulatum causing intracellular yeast in macrophages
options.rationale: Histoplasma shows intracellular yeasts in macrophages on tissue stains and is associated with bird/bat guano, but India ink capsule is characteristic of Cryptococcus.

question: Which laboratory test is more sensitive than India ink for detecting the polysaccharide capsule of the organism causing meningitis in AIDS patients?
cited_learning_objective: Explain the clinical manifestations, diagnosis, and treatment of Cryptococcus infections.

options.text: Capsular antigen detection by latex agglutination assay
options.rationale: Lecture notes that capsular antigen detection via latex agglutination is more sensitive and specific than India ink for Cryptococcus diagnosis.

options.text: Blood culture for mucoid yeast colonies
options.rationale: Culture is definitive but slower and less sensitive initially than antigen detection; antigen testing is more sensitive for rapid diagnosis.

options.text: PCR amplification of fungal ribosomal DNA
options.rationale: PCR can detect fungal DNA but the lecture highlights capsular antigen assays as the more sensitive routine test compared to India ink.

options.text: Mucicarmine stain of sputum for red-staining capsule
options.rationale: Mucicarmine stains the capsule in tissue but is less sensitive than antigen detection in CSF for diagnosing cryptococcal meningitis.

options.text: KOH preparation of CSF to visualize fungal elements
options.rationale: KOH prep is used for superficial fungal specimens; antigen detection in CSF is the preferred sensitive test for cryptococcal meningitis.

question: According to the lecture, what is the recommended induction therapy for symptomatic cryptococcal meningitis in an immunocompromised patient?
cited_learning_objective: Explain the clinical manifestations, diagnosis, and treatment of Cryptococcus infections.

options.text: Amphotericin B plus flucytosine followed by fluconazole
options.rationale: Lecture specifies amphotericin B with flucytosine for symptomatic disease induction, followed by maintenance fluconazole for cryptococcal meningitis.

options.text: Fluconazole monotherapy for induction and maintenance
options.rationale: Fluconazole alone may be used for mild disease or maintenance, but induction for symptomatic meningitis requires amphotericin B plus flucytosine.

options.text: Itraconazole combined with terbinafine for severe disease
options.rationale: Itraconazole and terbinafine are not the recommended induction regimen for cryptococcal meningitis per the lecture.

options.text: Voriconazole monotherapy followed by amphotericin B
options.rationale: Voriconazole is not the standard induction therapy; amphotericin B plus flucytosine is the lecture-recommended initial regimen.

options.text: Echinocandin therapy combined with fluconazole
options.rationale: Echinocandins are ineffective against Cryptococcus; they are not part of the recommended treatment for cryptococcal meningitis.

question: A patient with advanced HIV presents with multiple ring-enhancing lesions on MRI and positive Toxoplasma IgG. According to the lecture, which initial therapy is most appropriate for suspected reactivation CNS disease?
cited_learning_objective: Explain the pathogenesis and clinical manifestations of HIV-associated neurocognitive disorders and the disease caused by reactivation of latent or acquisition of opportunistic pathogens.

options.text: Pyrimethamine plus sulfadiazine with corticosteroids as needed
options.rationale: Lecture indicates reactivated CNS toxoplasmosis in immunocompromised patients is treated with pyrimethamine-sulfadiazine and corticosteroids to reduce cerebral edema.

options.text: Immediate amphotericin B and flucytosine combination therapy
options.rationale: Amphotericin B with flucytosine treats cryptococcal meningitis, not toxoplasmic brain abscesses; imaging and serology guide therapy selection.

options.text: High-dose fluconazole monotherapy for presumed fungal abscess
options.rationale: Fluconazole targets fungal infections like Cryptococcus; toxoplasmosis requires antiparasitic therapy such as pyrimethamine-sulfadiazine.

options.text: Empiric albendazole and praziquantel for helminthic infection
options.rationale: Albendazole/praziquantel treat helminths like Taenia; they are not appropriate for Toxoplasma gondii reactivation in CNS.

options.text: Observation without therapy pending brain biopsy
options.rationale: Delaying therapy is unsafe in suspected toxoplasmic encephalitis; empiric antiparasitic treatment is indicated while confirming diagnosis.

question: Which imaging clue on MRI helps distinguish progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy from toxoplasmic brain lesions in an HIV patient, per the lecture?
cited_learning_objective: Explain the pathogenesis and clinical manifestations of HIV-associated neurocognitive disorders and the disease caused by reactivation of latent or acquisition of opportunistic pathogens.

options.text: Non-enhancing demyelinating lesions suggestive of JC virus reactivation
options.rationale: Lecture notes that PML from JC virus shows non-enhancing demyelinating lesions on MRI, distinguishing it from ring-enhancing toxoplasma abscesses.

options.text: Multiple ring-enhancing lesions typical of toxoplasmosis
options.rationale: Ring-enhancing lesions are characteristic of toxoplasmosis, not PML; this option describes toxoplasma rather than the distinguishing PML pattern.

options.text: Single ring-enhancing lesion indicating CNS lymphoma
options.rationale: CNS lymphoma often presents as a solitary ring-enhancing lesion; this does not describe the non-enhancing demyelination of PML.

options.text: Diffuse meningeal enhancement consistent with cryptococcal meningitis
options.rationale: Cryptococcal meningitis typically affects meninges and may show meningeal enhancement, not the focal non-enhancing white matter lesions of PML.

options.text: Multiple calcified parenchymal cysts indicating helminthic disease
options.rationale: Calcified cysts suggest neurocysticercosis from Taenia solium, not the demyelinating lesions seen in PML.

question: Which opportunistic pathogen is most commonly associated with meningitis in patients with AIDS and is acquired from environmental bird droppings?
cited_learning_objective: Describe the physiological properties, virulence factors, and transmission of Cryptococcus spp.

options.text: Cryptococcus neoformans from bird-contaminated soil
options.rationale: Lecture states C. neoformans is associated with bird droppings and commonly causes meningitis in immunocompromised patients.

options.text: Cryptococcus gattii from eucalyptus-associated soil
options.rationale: C. gattii is associated with trees and can infect immunocompetent hosts; C. neoformans is the species most linked to AIDS-related meningitis.

options.text: Pneumocystis jirovecii causing pulmonary infection
options.rationale: Pneumocystis causes pneumonia in AIDS patients, not the meningitis associated with bird-dropping–linked Cryptococcus neoformans.

options.text: Histoplasma capsulatum from bat guano exposure
options.rationale: Histoplasma is linked to bat guano and can disseminate but is not the classic cause of meningitis from bird droppings described in the lecture.

options.text: Candida albicans from endogenous mucosal colonization
options.rationale: Candida can cause systemic disease but is not the environmental bird-dropping–associated meningitis pathogen emphasized in the lecture.

question: A 28-year-old presents with fever, stiff neck, and severe headache. CSF shows opening pressure 250 mm H2O [60-180], WBC 1200/mm3 [0-5] with 85% neutrophils, glucose 30 mg/dL [≥60], and protein 150 mg/dL [≤30]. According to the CSF profiles in the lecture, which category best fits these findings?
cited_learning_objective: Describe the typical CSF profile observed in fungal and mycobacterium CNS infections and how this profile differs from infections caused by viruses or bacteria.

options.text: Pyogenic bacterial meningitis with elevated neutrophils
options.rationale: High opening pressure, neutrophilic pleocytosis, low glucose, and high protein match the CSF profile of pyogenic bacterial infection described in the lecture.

options.text: Viral meningitis with lymphocytic predominance
options.rationale: Viral CSF typically shows lymphocytic predominance, normal glucose, and only mildly elevated protein, unlike the neutrophilic, low-glucose profile here.

options.text: Fungal or mycobacterial infection with lymphocytes and low glucose
options.rationale: Fungal/mycobacterial CSF can show lymphocytic predominance and low glucose but usually less marked neutrophilia and different opening pressure patterns than pyogenic bacterial meningitis.

options.text: Normal CSF parameters without infection
options.rationale: These values are far outside normal ranges and indicate an infectious process, not normal CSF.

options.text: Prion disease with elevated 14-3-3 protein only
options.rationale: Prion disease may show elevated 14-3-3 protein but does not produce this neutrophilic, low-glucose CSF profile typical of bacterial meningitis.

question: Which CSF pattern in the lecture is most consistent with fungal or mycobacterial CNS infection?
cited_learning_objective: Describe the typical CSF profile observed in fungal and mycobacterium CNS infections and how this profile differs from infections caused by viruses or bacteria.

options.text: Normal or mildly increased opening pressure with lymphocytic pleocytosis and low glucose
options.rationale: Lecture indicates mycobacterial and fungal infections often show normal or mildly increased opening pressure, lymphocytic WBCs, low glucose, and elevated protein.

options.text: Markedly elevated opening pressure with neutrophilic pleocytosis and very low glucose
options.rationale: This pattern describes pyogenic bacterial meningitis rather than fungal or mycobacterial infections.

options.text: Normal opening pressure with neutrophils and normal glucose
options.rationale: Neutrophilic predominance with normal glucose is not characteristic of fungal/mycobacterial CNS infections.

options.text: Very low protein with lymphocytic predominance and high glucose
options.rationale: Fungal/mycobacterial infections typically have elevated protein and low glucose, not low protein and high glucose.

options.text: Elevated opening pressure with prion-specific 14-3-3 protein only
options.rationale: Prion disease is not defined by CSF pleocytosis or low glucose; 14-3-3 is supportive but not the CSF pattern described for fungal/mycobacterial infections.

question: Which clinical sign is most specific for meningeal irritation and is listed in the lecture as part of the classic triad for meningitis?
cited_learning_objective: Clinical manifestations of meningitis and encephalitis

options.text: Neck stiffness or nuchal rigidity on examination
options.rationale: The lecture lists severe headache, stiff neck (nuchal rigidity), and high fever as the classic triad for meningitis; neck stiffness is a specific meningeal sign.

options.text: Altered mental status with personality change
options.rationale: Altered mental status and personality changes are more characteristic of encephalitis rather than the classic meningitis triad.

options.text: Focal motor weakness without systemic symptoms
options.rationale: Focal deficits can occur but are not part of the classic meningitis triad emphasized in the lecture.

options.text: Photophobia without headache or fever
options.rationale: Photophobia may accompany meningitis but is not one of the three classic triad features highlighted in the lecture.

options.text: Peripheral neuropathy with sensory loss
options.rationale: Peripheral neuropathy is not a classic sign of meningitis and is not included in the triad discussed in the lecture.

question: A patient with suspected neurosyphilis demonstrates pupils that accommodate but do not react to light. Which specific sign described in the lecture does this finding represent?
cited_learning_objective: Explain the pathogenesis and key symptoms of early and late neurosyphilis.

options.text: Argyll Robertson pupil characteristic of tertiary neurosyphilis
options.rationale: Lecture describes Argyll Robertson pupils as accommodating but nonreactive to light, a classic sign of late neurosyphilis.

options.text: Marcus Gunn pupil indicating afferent pupillary defect
options.rationale: Marcus Gunn pupil is an afferent pupillary defect seen in optic nerve disease, not the accommodate-without-react feature of Argyll Robertson pupil.

options.text: Horner syndrome with ptosis and miosis
options.rationale: Horner syndrome causes ptosis and miosis due to sympathetic disruption, not the specific accommodation-without-light reaction of Argyll Robertson pupil.

options.text: Adie tonic pupil with slow constriction to light
options.rationale: Adie pupil shows tonic, slow constriction and is not the classic neurosyphilis Argyll Robertson finding.

options.text: Argyll Robertson pupil due to ocular cryptococcal infection
options.rationale: Argyll Robertson pupil is specific to neurosyphilis pathophysiology, not cryptococcal ocular disease.

question: Which serologic test is appropriate for initial screening of syphilis and is expected to decline after successful therapy, as described in the lecture?
cited_learning_objective: Review of Serological Tests for syphilis

options.text: Rapid plasma reagin (RPR) non-treponemal screening test
options.rationale: Lecture states RPR (a non-treponemal test) is used for screening and its titers decline after effective treatment.

options.text: FTA-ABS treponemal confirmatory antibody assay
options.rationale: FTA-ABS is a treponemal confirmatory test that remains positive for life and does not reliably decline after treatment.

options.text: TP-PA treponemal particle agglutination assay
options.rationale: TP-PA is a specific treponemal test used for confirmation and typically remains positive despite treatment.

options.text: Dark-field microscopy of lesion exudate
options.rationale: Dark-field microscopy directly visualizes spirochetes in primary lesions but is not a serologic screening test and cannot track treatment response.

options.text: HIV antibody screening test for co-infection
options.rationale: HIV testing is important in STI evaluation but is not a syphilis serologic screening test and does not reflect syphilis treatment response.

question: A patient with suspected early neurosyphilis has CSF positive for spirochetes on dark-field microscopy. Which stage of syphilis does dark-field microscopy most reliably detect according to the lecture?
cited_learning_objective: Review of Serological Tests for syphilis

options.text: Primary and secondary stages when lesions are present
options.rationale: Lecture indicates dark-field or DFA microscopy directly detects spirochetes in primary chancres and secondary lesions when organisms are abundant.

options.text: Latent stage without mucocutaneous lesions
options.rationale: Dark-field microscopy requires visible lesions to sample; latent syphilis lacks such lesions and is diagnosed serologically.

options.text: Tertiary neurosyphilis with gummatous lesions only
options.rationale: Tertiary disease is often paucibacillary and dark-field microscopy is less useful; serology and clinical features guide diagnosis.

options.text: Congenital syphilis in neonates by blood smear
options.rationale: Congenital syphilis diagnosis relies on serology and clinical findings; dark-field microscopy is not the standard neonatal screening method.

options.text: All stages equally with high sensitivity
options.rationale: Dark-field microscopy is most sensitive in early stages with active lesions and is not equally sensitive across all stages.

question: Which virulence factor of Treponema pallidum facilitates perivascular infiltration and tissue spread as noted in the lecture?
cited_learning_objective: Review the biology of Treponema pallidum.

options.text: Hyaluronidase enzyme promoting tissue invasion
options.rationale: Lecture lists hyaluronidase as a virulence factor that facilitates perivascular infiltration and tissue spread of T. pallidum.

options.text: Polysaccharide capsule preventing phagocytosis
options.rationale: T. pallidum lacks a prominent polysaccharide capsule; fibronectin coating, not a capsule, contributes to anti-phagocytic properties.

options.text: Endotoxin-mediated septic shock from LPS
options.rationale: Spirochetes like T. pallidum are not Gram-negative with LPS endotoxin; endotoxin-mediated shock is not a described virulence mechanism here.

options.text: Beta-lactamase production conferring penicillin resistance
options.rationale: T. pallidum remains penicillin-sensitive and does not rely on beta-lactamase production; penicillin is effective therapy.

options.text: Spore formation enabling environmental persistence
options.rationale: T. pallidum does not form spores; hyaluronidase and fibronectin coat are the virulence features emphasized in the lecture.

question: A 38-year-old with HIV presents with a solitary, ring-enhancing brain lesion on MRI. Biopsy reveals B-cell lymphoma. Which latent virus reactivation discussed in the lecture is most commonly implicated in this CNS lymphoma?
cited_learning_objective: The basic virus characteristics of & diseases caused by: CMV, EBV, & JC virus

options.text: Epstein-Barr virus reactivation causing B-cell lymphoma
options.rationale: Lecture notes that EBV reactivation can lead to B-cell lymphomas including CNS lymphoma, often presenting as a solitary ring-enhancing lesion.

options.text: JC virus reactivation causing demyelinating lesions
options.rationale: JC virus causes PML with non-enhancing demyelinating lesions, not solitary ring-enhancing B-cell lymphoma.

options.text: Cytomegalovirus reactivation causing encephalitis
options.rationale: CMV can cause encephalitis and other systemic disease in AIDS but is not the classic cause of EBV-associated B-cell CNS lymphoma.

options.text: Human herpesvirus 6 causing temporal lobe encephalitis
options.rationale: HHV-6 can cause encephalitis but was not highlighted in the lecture as the cause of CNS B-cell lymphoma.

options.text: HIV direct infection causing primary CNS lymphoma
options.rationale: HIV contributes to immunosuppression but EBV reactivation specifically drives many AIDS-related primary CNS B-cell lymphomas.

question: Which opportunistic infection mnemonic from the lecture lists common CMV clinical sites using the acronym CREEP?
cited_learning_objective: The basic virus characteristics of & diseases caused by: CMV, EBV, & JC virus

options.text: CREEP: Colitis, Retinitis, Esophagitis, Encephalitis, Pneumonitis
options.rationale: Lecture provides the CREEP mnemonic for CMV manifestations: Colitis, Retinitis, Esophagitis, Encephalitis, and Pneumonitis.

options.text: CNS: Cryptococcal, Rabies, Enterovirus, Polio
options.rationale: This option mixes unrelated pathogens and does not reflect the CMV CREEP mnemonic discussed in the lecture.

options.text: CRIME: Carditis, Retinitis, Immunodeficiency, Encephalitis
options.rationale: CRIME is not the mnemonic used in the lecture; the correct CMV mnemonic is CREEP.

options.text: CREAM: Colitis, Retinitis, Esophagitis, Aortitis, Meningitis
options.rationale: CREAM is not the lecture mnemonic and includes aortitis which is not a typical CMV manifestation listed in the lecture.

options.text: CLOTS: Colitis, Lymphadenopathy, Ocular disease, Thrombosis, Sepsis
options.rationale: CLOTS is not the mnemonic presented; the lecture specifically used CREEP for CMV clinical sites.

question: Which of the following best describes the life cycle stage of Toxoplasma gondii that is most resistant and commonly found in brain tissue, as described in the lecture?
cited_learning_objective: Toxoplasma gondii; transmission, pathogenesis, clinical manifestation, diagnosis, & treatment of toxoplasmosis

options.text: Bradyzoite-containing tissue cysts that persist in brain and muscle
options.rationale: Lecture explains tissue cysts with bradyzoites are resistant, commonly located in brain and muscle, and can reactivate in immunosuppression.

options.text: Sporozoites in oocysts shed only by humans
options.rationale: Sporozoites are in oocysts shed by felids (definitive hosts), not humans; bradyzoite tissue cysts are the persistent form in humans.

options.text: Tachyzoites forming extracellular cysts in blood
options.rationale: Tachyzoites are the rapidly dividing invasive form, not the resistant bradyzoite tissue cysts that persist in brain tissue.

options.text: Metacercariae encysted in undercooked meat
options.rationale: Metacercariae refer to trematode life stages, not Toxoplasma; the lecture identifies bradyzoite tissue cysts as the resistant stage.

options.text: Oocyst-derived trophozoites that invade intestinal mucosa
options.rationale: Oocysts can lead to infection, but the resistant brain form emphasized in the lecture is the bradyzoite-containing tissue cyst.

question: In an AIDS patient with multiple ring-enhancing brain lesions, which serologic finding in the lecture supports reactivation of the intracellular protozoan discussed?
cited_learning_objective: Toxoplasma gondii; transmission, pathogenesis, clinical manifestation, diagnosis, & treatment of toxoplasmosis

options.text: Positive IgG antibodies to Toxoplasma gondii indicating prior exposure
options.rationale: Lecture notes that positive T. gondii IgG in an immunocompromised patient with ring-enhancing lesions supports reactivation of latent infection.

options.text: Positive IgM antibodies indicating acute primary infection
options.rationale: IgM indicates recent infection; in reactivation scenarios in AIDS patients, IgG positivity with clinical/radiologic findings is more typical.

options.text: Negative serology excluding toxoplasmosis definitively
options.rationale: Negative serology does not definitively exclude toxoplasmosis, especially in immunocompromised patients; lecture emphasizes IgG positivity as a clue.

options.text: Positive cryptococcal antigen in serum
options.rationale: Cryptococcal antigen indicates cryptococcosis, not toxoplasmosis; ring-enhancing lesions with positive Toxoplasma IgG point to toxoplasma reactivation.

options.text: PCR detection of JC virus DNA in CSF
options.rationale: JC virus PCR supports PML diagnosis, which shows non-enhancing demyelination, not multiple ring-enhancing toxoplasma lesions.

question: Which antifolate combination is the lecture-recommended therapy for severe CNS disease due to the intracellular protozoan that reactivates in AIDS patients?
cited_learning_objective: Toxoplasma gondii; transmission, pathogenesis, clinical manifestation, diagnosis, & treatment of toxoplasmosis

options.text: Pyrimethamine plus sulfadiazine for CNS toxoplasmosis
options.rationale: Lecture states pyrimethamine-sulfadiazine is used to treat disseminated or CNS toxoplasmosis by inhibiting folate synthesis.

options.text: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole as first-line induction therapy
options.rationale: TMP-SMX can be used for prophylaxis and sometimes treatment, but the lecture highlights pyrimethamine-sulfadiazine for CNS toxoplasmosis.

options.text: Metronidazole combined with amphotericin B
options.rationale: Metronidazole targets anaerobes and some protozoa but is not the standard therapy for CNS toxoplasmosis; amphotericin B is antifungal.

options.text: Albendazole and praziquantel for protozoal brain abscesses
options.rationale: Albendazole and praziquantel treat helminths like Taenia, not intracellular protozoa such as Toxoplasma gondii.

options.text: Fluconazole monotherapy for protozoal encephalitis
options.rationale: Fluconazole is antifungal and not effective against Toxoplasma; pyrimethamine-sulfadiazine is the lecture-recommended antiparasitic regimen.

question: Which statement best contrasts taeniasis and cysticercosis based on the lecture?
cited_learning_objective: Compare & contrast the transmission, diagnosis, & treatment of taeniasis vs. neurocysticercosis.

options.text: Taeniasis follows ingestion of larval cysts; cysticercosis follows ingestion of eggs
options.rationale: Lecture emphasizes taeniasis results from ingesting cysticerci in undercooked meat, whereas cysticercosis results from ingesting eggs via fecal-oral contamination.

options.text: Both conditions are caused by beef tapeworm ingestion only
options.rationale: T. saginata (beef) causes taeniasis but does not cause cysticercosis; only T. solium eggs cause cysticercosis.

options.text: Cysticercosis is diagnosed by stool ova detection exclusively
options.rationale: Stool exam diagnoses intestinal taeniasis; neurocysticercosis is primarily diagnosed by imaging and sometimes serology, not stool alone.

options.text: Taeniasis always causes neurologic symptoms and seizures
options.rationale: Taeniasis is usually intestinal and often asymptomatic; neurocysticercosis causes neurologic symptoms like seizures when cysts are in the CNS.

options.text: Both are prevented only by freezing meat at −20°C
options.rationale: Freezing pork prevents taeniasis from cysticerci, but preventing cysticercosis requires sanitation to avoid egg ingestion; freezing alone is insufficient.

question: Which of the following best describes the recommended prevention strategy for Naegleria fowleri exposure highlighted in the lecture?
cited_learning_objective: Explain the transmission, pathogenesis, clinical manifestation, & diagnosis of infections caused by Naegleria fowleri.

options.text: Avoid warm freshwater and use sterile water for nasal irrigation
options.rationale: Lecture recommends avoiding warm freshwater, using nose clips, and using filtered or distilled water for nasal irrigation to prevent Naegleria infection.

options.text: Boil drinking water to prevent ingestion-related disease
options.rationale: Boiling drinking water prevents ingestion-borne pathogens but Naegleria requires nasal exposure; the lecture emphasizes nasal precautions and avoiding warm freshwater.

options.text: Vaccination prior to freshwater exposure in endemic areas
options.rationale: No vaccine exists for Naegleria; prevention focuses on behavioral measures and water treatment as per the lecture.

options.text: Prophylactic oral miltefosine before swimming
options.rationale: Miltefosine is used therapeutically in combination regimens, not as prophylaxis; prevention is behavioral and environmental.

options.text: Regular antifungal nasal sprays to reduce colonization
options.rationale: Antifungal nasal sprays are not recommended for Naegleria prevention; the lecture advises avoiding nasal exposure to warm freshwater.

question: Which of the following best characterizes prion infectivity and resistance as emphasized in the lecture?
cited_learning_objective: Define the basic biochemical and biological properties of prions and compare the structural and functional significances of PrPc and PrPSc.

options.text: Prions are proteinaceous agents highly resistant to standard decontamination
options.rationale: Lecture highlights prions (PrPSc) as proteinaceous infectious agents that are highly resistant to chemicals, heat, UV, and proteases, requiring stringent decontamination.

options.text: Prions contain nucleic acids that are easily degraded by proteases
options.rationale: Prions lack nucleic acids; their resistance to proteases is a hallmark of PrPSc, not easy degradation.

options.text: PrPSc is rapidly turned over in hours like PrPc
options.rationale: PrPSc has slower turnover measured in days, whereas PrPc turns over in hours as noted in the lecture.

options.text: Prion infectivity is eliminated by routine autoclaving at standard settings
options.rationale: Lecture states prions require prolonged autoclaving (121°C, 15 psi for 4.5 hours) beyond routine settings to inactivate.

options.text: PrPc and PrPSc differ by primary amino acid sequence
options.rationale: PrPc and PrPSc share identical primary sequence; they differ in tertiary structure and properties, per the lecture.

question: A patient with suspected cryptococcal meningitis has a positive serum cryptococcal antigen but negative CSF India ink. According to the lecture, what is the next best interpretation?
cited_learning_objective: Explain the clinical manifestations, diagnosis, and treatment of Cryptococcus infections.

options.text: Positive serum antigen supports diagnosis despite negative India ink
options.rationale: Lecture notes antigen detection is more sensitive than India ink; a positive serum cryptococcal antigen supports cryptococcal infection even if India ink is negative.

options.text: Negative India ink rules out cryptococcal meningitis definitively
options.rationale: India ink misses about half of cases; a negative result does not definitively exclude cryptococcal meningitis when antigen is positive.

options.text: Start albendazole for presumed helminthic CNS disease
options.rationale: Albendazole treats helminths; a positive cryptococcal antigen indicates fungal infection requiring antifungal therapy, not anti-helminthics.

options.text: Order PCR for JC virus as the most likely cause
options.rationale: JC virus causes PML with different imaging and clinical features; positive cryptococcal antigen points toward cryptococcal disease rather than JC virus.

options.text: Treat empirically with high-dose corticosteroids alone
options.rationale: Corticosteroids alone are inappropriate; cryptococcal meningitis requires antifungal induction therapy, and steroids may be adjunctive for edema.

question: Which of the following best summarizes the lecture's guidance on antiretroviral therapy (ART) initiation for HIV-positive patients?
cited_learning_objective: ALL HIV+ patients should be started and take antiretroviral therapy (ART) the rest of their lives

options.text: Initiate lifelong combination ART at time of HIV diagnosis
options.rationale: Lecture states all HIV-positive patients should start and continue combination ART lifelong, typically two NRTIs plus another agent.

options.text: Delay ART until CD4 count falls below 200 cells/uL
options.rationale: Lecture recommends initiating ART at diagnosis regardless of CD4 count, not waiting until CD4 <200.

options.text: Use monotherapy with a single NRTI initially
options.rationale: Monotherapy is inadequate; lecture recommends combination therapy (at least three drugs) for effective viral suppression.

options.text: Start ART only after opportunistic infections are fully treated
options.rationale: While timing may be individualized, lecture emphasizes initiating ART at diagnosis rather than delaying until after treatment of opportunistic infections.

options.text: Prescribe ART intermittently based on viral load spikes
options.rationale: Intermittent ART is not recommended; continuous lifelong therapy is advised to maintain viral suppression per the lecture.

question: Which CNS pathogen discussed in the lecture is described as 'free-living' in warm freshwater and can present with an altered sense of smell early in disease?
cited_learning_objective: Explain the transmission, pathogenesis, clinical manifestation, & diagnosis of infections caused by Naegleria fowleri.

options.text: Naegleria fowleri, a free-living warm-water amoeba
options.rationale: Lecture describes Naegleria fowleri as a free-living amoeba in warm freshwater that can cause early anosmia or altered smell during infection.

options.text: Toxoplasma gondii transmitted by cat feces
options.rationale: Toxoplasma is intracellular and associated with cats; it does not present with acute anosmia from freshwater exposure.

options.text: Cryptococcus neoformans from bird droppings
options.rationale: Cryptococcus is environmental but associated with bird droppings and causes meningitis, not the acute freshwater-associated anosmia described.

options.text: Taenia solium causing neurocysticercosis
options.rationale: Taenia solium causes neurocysticercosis via egg ingestion and delayed seizures, not acute freshwater-associated olfactory symptoms.

options.text: JC virus causing progressive demyelination
options.rationale: JC virus causes PML with demyelination, not the acute, rapidly fatal freshwater-associated meningoencephalitis with altered smell.

question: Which of the following best explains why neurocysticercosis symptoms often arise when encysted larvae die, according to the lecture?
cited_learning_objective: Explain the pathogenesis of neurocysticercosis.

options.text: Host inflammatory response to degenerating cysticerci provokes symptoms
options.rationale: Lecture explains neurologic symptoms result from the inflammatory response when encysted Taenia solium larvae die and provoke local inflammation.

options.text: Active larval feeding causes continuous neuronal destruction
options.rationale: Symptoms are more associated with host inflammation during larval death rather than continuous feeding by live cysticerci.

options.text: Larval secretion of neurotoxins during growth triggers seizures
options.rationale: The lecture attributes symptoms to inflammation on cyst death rather than larval neurotoxin secretion during growth.

options.text: Direct bacterial superinfection of cysts causes acute symptoms
options.rationale: Bacterial superinfection is not the typical mechanism; inflammatory response to dying cysticerci is the primary cause of symptoms.

options.text: Autoimmune cross-reactivity to host myelin causes delayed symptoms
options.rationale: While immune mechanisms are involved, the lecture specifically highlights inflammation from dying cysticerci rather than autoimmune myelin cross-reactivity.

question: Which of the following best matches the lecture description of the three stages in the Naegleria fowleri life cycle relevant to diagnosis?
cited_learning_objective: Explain the transmission, pathogenesis, clinical manifestation, & diagnosis of infections caused by Naegleria fowleri.

options.text: Cyst, ameboid trophozoite, and temporary flagellate forms
options.rationale: Lecture describes Naegleria life stages as cysts, infective ameboid trophozoites (seen in CSF), and transient flagellate forms.

options.text: Egg, larva, and adult worm stages in freshwater
options.rationale: Egg/larva/adult stages describe helminths, not Naegleria; the amoeba has cyst, trophozoite, and flagellate stages.

options.text: Sporozoite, merozoite, and gametocyte stages in blood
options.rationale: These stages pertain to apicomplexan parasites like Plasmodium, not Naegleria fowleri.

options.text: Trophozoite, bradyzoite, and oocyst stages in tissue
options.rationale: Bradyzoite and oocyst stages are characteristic of Toxoplasma, not Naegleria's cyst/trophozoite/flagellate cycle.

options.text: Flagellated adult, cystic larva, and spore forms
options.rationale: This mixed description does not match Naegleria's specific life stages as outlined in the lecture.

question: Which of the following best reflects the lecture's statement about the epidemiology of neurocysticercosis worldwide?
cited_learning_objective: Explain the transmission, pathogenesis, clinical manifestation, diagnosis, & treatment of infections caused by Taenia solium.

options.text: It is the most common parasitic CNS infection globally but remains relatively rare
options.rationale: Lecture notes neurocysticercosis is the most common parasitic CNS infection worldwide yet overall rare, with millions affected in endemic regions.

options.text: It is common in developed countries and rare in endemic rural areas
options.rationale: Neurocysticercosis is more prevalent in rural endemic regions and among immigrants from those areas, not primarily in developed countries.

options.text: Taenia saginata is the primary cause of neurocysticercosis worldwide
options.rationale: T. saginata does not cause cysticercosis; T. solium is the species responsible for neurocysticercosis.

options.text: Transmission is mainly via mosquito vectors in endemic zones
options.rationale: Transmission occurs via ingestion of eggs in fecal-oral contamination, not via insect vectors.

options.text: It is eradicated globally through meat inspection programs
options.rationale: While meat inspection reduces taeniasis, cysticercosis persists due to fecal-oral transmission and sanitation issues; it is not eradicated globally.

question: Which of the following best summarizes the lecture's guidance on decontamination when handling tissues potentially contaminated with prions?
cited_learning_objective: Define the basic biochemical and biological properties of prions and compare the structural and functional significances of PrPc and PrPSc.

options.text: Use stringent decontamination; prolonged autoclaving at 121°C for 4.5 hours
options.rationale: Lecture emphasizes prions are highly resistant and require stringent decontamination such as autoclaving at 121°C, 15 psi for 4.5 hours.

options.text: Standard chemical disinfectants and brief autoclaving suffice
options.rationale: Routine disinfection and standard autoclaving are inadequate for prions; the lecture specifies prolonged high-temperature autoclaving.

options.text: Irradiation with UV light reliably inactivates prions
options.rationale: Prions are resistant to UV radiation; the lecture notes UV is ineffective and stringent methods are required.

options.text: Soaking instruments in alcohol for one hour eliminates infectivity
options.rationale: Alcohol is insufficient to inactivate prions; lecture recommends more rigorous decontamination protocols.

options.text: Freezing contaminated instruments prevents prion transmission
options.rationale: Freezing does not inactivate prions; lecture highlights their resistance and the need for stringent heat-based decontamination.

question: A patient with HIV has diffuse micronodules and ventricular enlargement on brain imaging and CSF PCR positive for a herpesvirus. Which virus from the lecture is most consistent with these findings and the acronym CREEP?
cited_learning_objective: The basic virus characteristics of & diseases caused by: CMV, EBV, & JC virus

options.text: Cytomegalovirus causing encephalitis and systemic disease
options.rationale: Lecture links CMV reactivation to encephalitis with diffuse micronodules and ventricular enlargement and lists CMV manifestations with the CREEP mnemonic.

options.text: Epstein-Barr virus causing primary demyelinating lesions
options.rationale: EBV is associated with B-cell lymphomas and CNS lymphoma, not the diffuse micronodular encephalitis pattern described for CMV.

options.text: JC virus causing non-enhancing demyelinating lesions
options.rationale: JC virus causes PML with non-enhancing demyelination, not the diffuse micronodular ventricular enlargement pattern linked to CMV.

options.text: Herpes simplex virus type 1 causing temporal lobe necrosis
options.rationale: HSV-1 causes temporal lobe encephalitis with focal necrosis, not the diffuse micronodular pattern associated with CMV in AIDS.

options.text: Varicella-zoster virus causing focal vasculopathy
options.rationale: VZV can cause vasculopathy and focal lesions but the lecture specifically associates CMV with the described diffuse CNS findings.

question: Which of the following best reflects the lecture's description of parasite classification distinguishing protozoa from metazoa?
cited_learning_objective: Parasite terminology: definitive host, intermediate host, dead-end host (see at end of slide deck); protozoan vs. metazoan; cestode vs. nematode vs. trematode

options.text: Protozoa are single-celled; metazoa are multicellular helminths
options.rationale: Lecture defines protozoa as single-celled organisms (faster onset) and metazoa (helminths) as multicellular with complex life cycles.

options.text: Metazoa are single-celled and form cysts in tissues
options.rationale: This reverses definitions; metazoa are multicellular helminths, whereas protozoa form cysts and trophozoites as single-celled organisms.

options.text: Protozoa always cause eosinophilia in blood tests
options.rationale: Eosinophilia is more typical of helminthic infections; protozoa often cause neutrophilic or lymphocytic responses depending on location.

options.text: Cestodes are roundworms with complete digestive tracts
options.rationale: Cestodes are tapeworms (flat, segmented) lacking a digestive tract; roundworms (nematodes) have complete digestive systems.

options.text: Trematodes are segmented tapeworms transmitted by undercooked meat
options.rationale: Trematodes are flukes (leaf-shaped), not segmented tapeworms; cestodes are tapeworms transmitted via undercooked meat.

question: Which of the following best describes the recommended role of corticosteroids in treating neurocysticercosis as stated in the lecture?
cited_learning_objective: Explain the transmission, pathogenesis, clinical manifestation, diagnosis, & treatment of infections caused by Taenia solium.

options.text: Use corticosteroids to reduce inflammation during antiparasitic therapy
options.rationale: Lecture recommends corticosteroids to minimize inflammatory response when treating cysticercosis with antiparasitic agents to reduce cerebral edema and symptoms.

options.text: Avoid corticosteroids because they increase parasite replication
options.rationale: Corticosteroids are used to control inflammation during treatment; the lecture does not advise avoiding them due to increased parasite replication.

options.text: Corticosteroids are the sole therapy for neurocysticercosis
options.rationale: Steroids are adjunctive to antiparasitic therapy and seizure management, not sole therapy for neurocysticercosis.

options.text: Use corticosteroids only after surgical removal of cysts
options.rationale: Steroids may be used perioperatively but lecture indicates they are used during medical antiparasitic treatment to control inflammation.

options.text: Corticosteroids prevent egg ingestion and transmission
options.rationale: Steroids do not prevent transmission; sanitation and treating carriers prevent egg ingestion as per the lecture.

question: Which of the following best matches the lecture's description of the typical clinical progression of Naegleria fowleri infection from exposure to death?
cited_learning_objective: Explain the transmission, pathogenesis, clinical manifestation, & diagnosis of infections caused by Naegleria fowleri.

options.text: Symptoms begin 1–12 days and often fatal within one to two weeks
options.rationale: Lecture states symptoms begin 1–12 days after exposure and the disease is nearly always fatal within about 1–2 weeks.

options.text: Incubation of months with chronic relapsing neurologic deficits
options.rationale: Naegleria causes an acute, rapidly progressive illness over days to weeks, not a chronic relapsing course.

options.text: Asymptomatic colonization for years before sudden decline
options.rationale: Naegleria infection is typically acute and severe shortly after exposure, not a long asymptomatic colonization.

options.text: Gradual cognitive decline over decades similar to prion disease
options.rationale: Prion diseases cause gradual decline over months to a year; Naegleria causes rapid fatal meningoencephalitis within weeks.

options.text: Intermittent febrile episodes with spontaneous recovery
options.rationale: Naegleria infection rarely results in spontaneous recovery and is usually rapidly progressive and fatal.

question: Which of the following best reflects the lecture's statement about the role of brain macrophages and microglia in HIV-associated neurocognitive disorders (HAND)?
cited_learning_objective: Explain the pathogenesis and clinical manifestations of HIV-associated neurocognitive disorders and the disease caused by reactivation of latent or acquisition of opportunistic pathogens.

options.text: Brain macrophages and microglia are key target cells in HAND pathogenesis
options.rationale: Lecture indicates brain macrophages and microglial infection and activation are central to HAND pathogenesis and neuronal injury.

options.text: Neurons are the primary site of productive HIV replication
options.rationale: Neurons are not the main productive HIV reservoir; microglia and macrophages are key CNS reservoirs implicated in HAND.

options.text: HAND results solely from opportunistic infections, not HIV itself
options.rationale: HAND can result from direct HIV effects on microglia/macrophages as well as opportunistic infections; it is not solely due to opportunists.

options.text: Astrocytes are the exclusive reservoir for HIV in the brain
options.rationale: Astrocytes may be involved but lecture emphasizes brain macrophages and microglia as key target cells, not exclusive astrocyte reservoirs.

options.text: Peripheral B cells mediate neuronal loss in HAND
options.rationale: Peripheral B cells are not the primary mediators of HAND; CNS macrophages and microglia play the central pathogenic role per the lecture.
